title: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) Calculator
metaDescription: Estimate your Basal Metabolic Rate using the Mifflin-St Jeor or Harris-Benedict equations, instantly in your browser.
shortDescription: Estimate daily calories burned at rest using standard BMR formulas.

guideSections.heading: Why Mifflin-St Jeor Is Generally Preferred Today
guideSections.body: The Harris-Benedict equation dates to 1919 and was revised in 1984, while Mifflin-St Jeor (1990) is generally considered more accurate for most modern populations based on later validation studies. Harris-Benedict tends to slightly overestimate BMR compared to Mifflin-St Jeor, which is why this tool offers both rather than picking one silently.

guideSections.heading: BMR Is Not the Same as Your Maintenance Calories
guideSections.body: BMR estimates energy burned at complete rest — it does not include calories burned through daily movement, exercise, or digestion. Using BMR alone as a calorie target without adding an activity multiplier will significantly underestimate actual daily energy needs for anyone who isn't bedridden.
